paras.text: Raat ke gyarah baje, {{A}} apni chhat pe akela baitha tha, phone haath me, {{B}} ka last message dobara padh raha tha — 'Aaj bahut mushkil din tha.' Usne call laga diya.

paras.speaker: A
paras.text: Kya hua, sab theek hai? Tumhara message padha, pareshan lagi.

paras.speaker: B
paras.text: (thaki awaaz me) Bas thoda mushkil din tha, exam ka pressure. Kaash tum yahan hote, sab kuch itna behtar lagta tumhare saath.

paras.speaker: A
paras.text: Mujhe bhi wahi feel ho raha hai. Waise ye 'kaash' wali baat — English me isko bahut khaas tarike se bolte hain, tumhe pata hai?

paras.speaker: B
paras.text: Haan, 'I wish' na? Bas kaise use karte hain thik se, kabhi confuse ho jaati hoon.

paras.speaker: A
paras.text: Chalo aaj isi pe baat karte hain, kyunki tumhari yehi feeling hai abhi. Pehle samjho — Subjunctive Mood ek special grammar form hai jo wishes, imaginary situations, aur formal suggestions ke liye use hoti hai — normal 'reality' wale rules yahan nahi chalte.

paras.speaker: B
paras.text: Achha, to 'I wish you were here' — ye sahi hai na? Maine kahin suna tha.

paras.speaker: A
paras.text: I wish you were here — bilkul sahi! Ye PRESENT wish hai, matlab abhi ki situation ke baare me afsos, jo sach nahi hai. Structure hai — 'I wish' + Past Simple, aur 'be' verb ke liye hamesha 'WERE', chahe subject koi bhi ho. 'I wish you were here' ka matlab — tum abhi yahan nahi ho, aur mujhe uska afsos hai.

paras.speaker: B
paras.text: Achha, ye to Conditional Type 2 jaisa lag raha hai, jo humne pehle seekha tha — 'were' wala rule same hai!

paras.speaker: A
paras.text: (khush hoke) Bilkul sahi connect kiya! Dono me similar logic hai — past form use karke 'unreal/imaginary' present dikhaya jaata hai. Chalo ek aur example dete hain, thoda alag context ka.

paras.speaker: B
paras.text: Haan bolo.

paras.speaker: A
paras.text: Chalo — 'Kaash mere paas zyada free time hota' → 'I wish I had more free time.' Ya 'Kaash mujhe uska number pata hota' → 'I wish I knew his number.' Dekho, dono me Past Simple use hua, present ki hi baat ho rahi hai, bas unreal/wish wali.

paras.speaker: B
paras.text: (notebook nikaal ke) Achha, ab batao — agar past ki kisi baat ka afsos ho, jaise 'kaash maine wo kiya hota' — wo bhi 'I wish' se hi banega?

paras.speaker: A
paras.text: Bilkul, bas structure thoda alag hoga — PAST wish ke liye.I wish I had studied harder for that exam — dekho, 'had + V3' use hua, bilkul Type 3 Conditional jaisa structure. 'I wish I had studied harder' ka matlab — maine nahi padha tha, aur ab uska pachtawa hai.

paras.speaker: B
paras.text: Ohh, matlab PRESENT wish me 'Past Simple' (were included), aur PAST wish me 'had + V3' — bilkul Conditional Type 2 aur Type 3 jaisa hi pattern hai!

paras.speaker: A
paras.text: Ekdum sahi socha! Ye connection yaad rakhoge to kabhi confuse nahi hoge. Chalo ab ek TEESRA type bhi hai 'I wish' ka, jo thoda alag hai — jab kisi ki HABIT ya BEHAVIOUR se annoyance ho, complaint karni ho.

paras.speaker: B
paras.text: Matlab? Ek example do na.

paras.speaker: A
paras.text: Chalo socho — maan lo tumhara roommate hamesha deer se aata hai raat ko, aur tumhe disturb karta hai. Tum bologi — 'Kaash wo itni der se na aaya kare' — matlab ek complaint, wish nahi sirf, thoda irritation bhi hai.I wish you wouldn't make so much noise — dekho, is type me 'would/wouldn't' use hota hai, aur ye SIRF doosron ke liye use hota hai, khud ke liye nahi — 'I wish I would...' nahi bolte.

paras.speaker: B
paras.text: (hasm ke) Achha, to teeno type clear ho gaye — Present wish (were/past simple), Past wish (had+V3), aur Annoyance wish (would/wouldn't, sirf doosron ke liye). Ye teeno me fark dekhne ka koi trick hai?

paras.speaker: A
paras.text: Bilkul — teen sawaal khud se poochho: (1) Kya ye ABHI ki baat hai jo sach nahi? Present wish. (2) Kya ye PAST ki baat hai jiska pachtawa hai? Past wish. (3) Kya ye kisi ke BEHAVIOUR se complaint hai? Annoyance wish, 'would' wala.

paras.speaker: B
paras.text: (pakka likhte hue) Achha, ab 'If only' ka kya scene hai? Wo bhi same hai kya 'I wish' jaisa?

paras.speaker: A
paras.text: Bilkul same structure, bas 'If only' thoda zyada EMOTIONAL/STRONG lagta hai 'I wish' se. Jaise — 'If only I had known earlier!' — ye sunne me zyada intense lagta hai 'I wish I had known earlier' se, though matlab same hai.

paras.speaker: B
paras.text: Achha, to main abhi bol sakti hoon — 'If only you were here right now.'

paras.speaker: A
paras.text: (narmi se muskurate hue) Perfect sentence, aur sach bhi. Chalo ab ek naya cheez seekhte hain, thoda related — 'As if' aur 'As though'. Ye bhi Subjunctive family ka hi hai.

paras.speaker: B
paras.text: Ye kaise use hota hai?

paras.speaker: A
paras.text: He talks as if he were rich — dekho, jab kisi ki HARKAT ko compare karte ho ek IMAGINARY/unreal situation se, tab 'as if/as though' use karte hain, aur usi 'were' wala rule follow hota hai.

paras.speaker: B
paras.text: Ek Hindi example do na, jisse main relate kar sakoon.

paras.speaker: A
paras.text: Chalo — 'Wo aise baat karta hai jaise use sab kuch pata ho' → 'He talks as if he knew everything.' Ya 'Wo aise react kar rahi thi jaise usne bhoot dekh liya ho' → 'She reacted as if she had seen a ghost.' Dekho, doosre wale me 'had + V3' use hua, kyunki ye PAST ki comparison hai.

paras.speaker: B
paras.text: (notebook me likhte hue) Achha, matlab yahan bhi present aur past dono versions hain, jaise 'I wish' me the. Ab batao, isme aur 'as if/as though' me koi fark hai?

paras.speaker: A
paras.text: Bilkul same matlab, bas 'as though' thoda zyada literary/formal lagta hai, roz ki baat-cheet me 'as if' zyada common hai. Dono chalenge exam me.

paras.speaker: B
paras.text: Achha, ab kuch aur bacha hai kya Subjunctive me? Ye topic to bahut interesting lag raha hai.

paras.speaker: A
paras.text: Haan, ek aakhri important part bacha hai — FORMAL Subjunctive. Ye thoda alag hai, official/formal English me use hota hai, jab koi ZAROORI suggestion, demand, ya requirement batani ho.

paras.speaker: B
paras.text: Ye kaise banta hai?

paras.speaker: A
paras.text: It is essential that he be present at the meeting — dekho, sabse important rule — verb hamesha BASE FORM me rehta hai, 'he be' hai, 'he is' ya 'he was' nahi — chahe subject third-person singular ho, chahe sentence past me ho, phir bhi base form.

paras.speaker: B
paras.text: (hairan) Kya?! 'He be' sunne me to galat lagta hai! Ye pakka sahi hai?

paras.speaker: A
paras.text: (hasm ke) Haan, ye SSC ka bahut common trap hai, kyunki sunne me ajeeb lagta hai, log turant 'he is' ya 'he was' likh dete hain, jo galat hai formal subjunctive me. Ye rule kaam karta hai in words ke saath — 'essential', 'necessary', 'important', 'suggest', 'recommend', 'demand', 'insist', 'propose'.

paras.speaker: B
paras.text: Ek aur example do na, taaki pakka yaad ho jaaye.

paras.speaker: A
paras.text: Chalo — 'The doctor recommended that he TAKE rest.' (na ki 'takes' ya 'took'). Aur — 'The manager demanded that the report BE submitted immediately.' (na ki 'is submitted' ya 'was submitted'). Dekho har jagah base form, chahe sentence ka baaki hissa present ho ya past.

paras.speaker: B
paras.text: Achha, ye 'essential/suggest/recommend/demand' to samajh gaya, but aur bhi words honge na jo isi tarah base-form maangte hain? Poori list do na, taaki exam me koi bhi word aaye, pehchan sakoon.

paras.speaker: A
paras.text: Bilkul, ye bahut achha sawaal hai — Present Subjunctive DO tarah ke trigger words se aata hai. Pehla group — VERBS: suggest, recommend, demand, insist, propose, request, require, urge, order, move (formal meeting me), desire, prefer, ask. Doosra group — 'It is + ADJECTIVE + that': essential, necessary, important, imperative, vital, crucial, desirable, urgent, obligatory. In sab ke baad, that-clause me verb hamesha BASE FORM.

paras.speaker: B
paras.text: (pakka likhte hue) Itni lambi list! Ek-ek karke example do na sabka, warna yaad nahi rahega.

paras.speaker: A
paras.text: Chalo, VERBS wale group se shuru karte hain — 'insist': 'She insisted that he APOLOGIZE immediately.' (na ki 'apologizes'). 'propose': 'I propose that the meeting BE postponed.' (na ki 'is postponed'). 'require': 'The rule requires that every member SIGN the form.' (na ki 'signs').

paras.speaker: B
paras.text: Achha, ye 'require' wala interesting hai, roz ki English me bhi use hota hoga shayad.

paras.speaker: A
paras.text: Bilkul, office/college notices me bahut common hai. Ek aur suno — 'urge': 'The doctor urged that the patient REST completely.' Aur roz ki baat-cheet wala, thoda casual — jaise tum apne boss ke baare me bologi, 'The boss wants that everybody COME on time.' Dekho, 'wants that' bhi isi pattern me aata hai, 'comes' nahi, 'come' hi.

paras.speaker: B
paras.text: (hairan) Sach me?! Maine to hamesha 'wants everyone to come' bola hai, 'wants that' wala pattern kabhi socha nahi tha subjunctive hoga!

paras.speaker: A
paras.text: Haan, dono sahi hain — 'The boss wants everyone to come on time' (infinitive wala, casual/common) aur 'The boss wants that everybody COME on time' (that-clause wala, formal/subjunctive). Exam me dono type ke sentence aa sakte hain, isliye 'wants that + base form' bhi pehchanna zaroori hai.

paras.speaker: B
paras.text: Achha, ab ADJECTIVE wala group batao — 'essential' to dekh chuke, baaki kaise use hote hain?

paras.speaker: A
paras.text: Chalo, sabse important wala pehle — IMPERATIVE. 'It is imperative that everybody COME on time.' Ye SSC me bahut common hai, 'imperative' ka matlab hota hai 'bahut zaroori/anivarya', aur pattern wahi hai — base form.

paras.speaker: B
paras.text: (likhte hue) 'It is imperative that...' — ye to bahut formal official notices me dikhta hai, ab samajh aaya kyu.

paras.speaker: A
paras.text: Ekdum sahi! Ab baaki adjectives ke example dekho — 'vital': 'It is vital that she ATTEND the interview.' 'crucial': 'It is crucial that the team FINISH the project on time.' 'desirable': 'It is desirable that all members BE present.' 'urgent': 'It is urgent that he CONTACT the office immediately.' 'obligatory': 'It is obligatory that every citizen CARRY an ID card.'

paras.speaker: B
paras.text: (sar pakadte hue, hasm ke) Itne saare! But pattern same hai sabme — 'It is + adjective + that + subject + BASE FORM', chahe adjective koi bhi ho.

paras.speaker: A
paras.text: Ekdum sahi pakda — yehi to sabse achhi baat hai, PATTERN hamesha same rehta hai, sirf trigger word badalta hai. Chalo ab isko test karte hain — main tumhe alag alag trigger words dunga, tum poora sentence banao base-form ke sath.

paras.speaker: B
paras.text: Chalo, ready hoon!

paras.speaker: A
paras.text: Pehla — 'imperative' — 'sab students exam hall me time pe pahunchein' isko banao.

paras.speaker: B
paras.text: (sochte hue) 'It is imperative that all students REACH the exam hall on time.'

paras.speaker: A
paras.text: Perfect! Doosra — 'insist' — 'teacher ne zor diya ki har student apna homework jama kare'.

paras.speaker: B
paras.text: 'The teacher insisted that every student SUBMIT their homework.'

paras.speaker: A
paras.text: Sahi! Teesra, wahi casual wala style — 'boss chahta hai ki sab meeting me time pe aayein' — 'wants that' use karke banao.

paras.speaker: B
paras.text: 'The boss wants that everybody COME to the meeting on time.'

paras.speaker: A
paras.text: (taali maarte hue) Perfect! Aakhri — 'vital' — 'ye zaroori hai ki hum jaldi decision lein'.

paras.speaker: B
paras.text: 'It is vital that we TAKE a quick decision.'

paras.speaker: A
paras.text: (muskurate hue) Full marks, phir se! Dekho, ab tumhe koi bhi trigger word (suggest/recommend/demand/insist/propose/require/urge/wants that, ya essential/necessary/important/imperative/vital/crucial/desirable/urgent/obligatory) dikhe, turant pata chal jaayega — base form aayega, 's' ya tense change nahi.

paras.speaker: B
paras.text: (pakka likhte hue) Ye bahut tricky hai but ab samajh aa gaya — in specific words ('essential/suggest/recommend/demand/insist/propose') ke baad verb hamesha base form.

paras.speaker: B
paras.text: Achha ek cheez pooch loon — exam paper me kabhi kabhi seedha likha hota hai 'identify the Present Subjunctive' ya 'Past Subjunctive' — ye exact terms kya hain? Humne jo abhi seekha unme se kaunsa kya hai?

paras.speaker: A
paras.text: Bahut zaroori sawaal, ye clear karna chahiye! In dono ke official (grammar-book) naam hote hain, chalo match karte hain — jo humne abhi 'Formal Subjunctive' bola (essential/suggest/recommend/demand + BASE FORM verb, jaise 'he BE', 'she TAKE'), uska official naam hai PRESENT SUBJUNCTIVE. Naam 'present' hai, but ismein tense se koi lena-dena nahi, bas hamesha base form use hota hai.

paras.speaker: B
paras.text: Achha, aur PAST SUBJUNCTIVE?

paras.speaker: A
paras.text: PAST SUBJUNCTIVE wahi 'WERE' wala rule hai jo humne 'I wish', 'if only', aur 'as if/as though' me dekha — jaise 'I wish I were rich' ya 'as if he were a king' ya 'If I were you'. Isko 'past' isliye kehte hain kyunki grammatically 'were' ek past-form hai, chahe baat PRESENT ki hi ho rahi ho — naam thoda confusing hai, but yaad rakhna: 'were' wala hamesha PAST Subjunctive.

paras.speaker: B
paras.text: (pakka likhte hue) Ohh, ab bilkul clear hai — PRESENT Subjunctive = base form (essential/suggest/recommend wala), PAST Subjunctive = 'were' (wish/as if/conditional Type 2 wala). Naam se confuse nahi hona, tense ka matlab nahi hai in naamon me.

paras.speaker: A
paras.text: Ekdum sahi! Ye exam me directly poocha jaata hai kabhi kabhi — 'Which sentence uses the Past Subjunctive?' — to ab tumhe pata hai dhoondhna hai 'were' (unreal wish/comparison me), 'Present Subjunctive' ke liye dhoondhna hai base-form verb (essential/suggest/demand ke baad).

paras.speaker: A
paras.text: Ekdum sahi! Ek chhota bonus bhi bata deta hoon — kuch FIXED expressions bhi hain jo subjunctive se bante hain, jinhe tum roz sunte ho, bina pehchane. Jaise 'God bless you!', 'Long live the King!', 'Heaven forbid!' — in sab me subjunctive form hai, base verb use hota hai, normal 's' nahi lagta.

paras.speaker: B
paras.text: (hairan) Sach me?! Maine kabhi socha nahi tha 'God bless you' subjunctive hai! Normal hota to 'God blesses you' hota na, third person singular rule se?

paras.speaker: A
paras.text: Ekdum sahi pakda! Yehi to interesting hai — ye ek prayer/wish hai, isliye subjunctive base form use hui, 'blesses' nahi. Similarly 'Long live the King' — normal grammar se 'lives' hona chahiye tha, but ye ek wish/prayer hai, isliye base form 'live' hi rehta hai.

paras.speaker: B
paras.text: (khush hoke) Wow, ye sab ab tak roz sunte the, kabhi socha nahi ye grammar ka naam kya hai! Chalo ab poora practice karte hain, sab mix karke.

paras.speaker: A
paras.text: Chalo, main paanch sentences dunga.

paras.speaker: B
paras.text: Ready hoon!

paras.speaker: A
paras.text: Pehla — 'Kaash mere paas ek car hoti.' (present wish)

paras.speaker: B
paras.text: 'I wish I had a car.'

paras.speaker: A
paras.text: Sahi! Doosra — 'Kaash maine tumhari baat suni hoti.' (past wish, regret)

paras.speaker: B
paras.text: 'I wish I had listened to you.' ya 'If only I had listened to you.'

paras.speaker: A
paras.text: Perfect! Teesra — 'Wo aise chal raha hai jaise usko koi jaldi ho.' (as if, present)

paras.speaker: B
paras.text: 'He is walking as if he were in a hurry.'

paras.speaker: A
paras.text: Ekdum sahi! Chautha — 'Principal ne suggest kiya ki har student time pe aaye.' (formal subjunctive)

paras.speaker: B
paras.text: (sochte hue) 'The Principal suggested that every student BE punctual.' ya 'that every student ARRIVE on time' — base form use kiya.

paras.speaker: A
paras.text: (taali maarte hue) Perfect! Aakhri — 'Kaash tum itni jaldi gussa na hua karo.' (annoyance, would wala)

paras.speaker: B
paras.text: 'I wish you wouldn't get angry so quickly.'

paras.speaker: A
paras.text: (muskurate hue) Full marks! Dekho, itna emotional topic tha aaj, aur tumne itni achhi tarah seekh liya.

paras.speaker: B
paras.text: (narmi se) Sach kahoon, isliye achha laga kyunki jo bhi seekha, wahi feel bhi kar rahi thi — 'I wish you were here' sach me mera dil se nikla tha.

paras.speaker: A
paras.text: (dhime se) Mera bhi. Bas kuch mahine aur — phir 'I wish' nahi, 'I am' bologe.
Interview hall ke bahar, wahi purani wish yaad aa rahi thi — sach me kabhi 'I wish' se 'I am' bana tha kya, ya wo bhi ek adhoora sapna reh gaya...

quiz.q: Fill (present wish): 'I wish I ___ (know) the answer to this question right now.'
quiz.explain: Present wish ke liye 'I wish' + Past Simple use hota hai — 'knew', 'know' nahi.

quiz.q: Fill (past wish, regret): 'I wish I ___ (study) harder before the exam.'
quiz.explain: Past wish/regret ke liye 'I wish' + had + V3 (past perfect) use hota hai.

quiz.q: Correct the error: 'I wish I would have a bigger house.' (talking about NOW, present wish)
quiz.answer: I wish I had a bigger house.
quiz.explain: 'I wish + would' sirf doosron ke behaviour/annoyance ke liye use hota hai, khud ke present wish ke liye nahi — wahan sirf Past Simple aata hai ('had').

quiz.q: Fill (as if, present unreal): 'She speaks English as if she ___ (be) a native speaker.'
quiz.explain: 'As if/as though' me 'be' verb ke liye hamesha 'were' aata hai, sab subjects ke saath, jaise Conditional Type 2 me.

quiz.q: Correct the error: 'It is important that he attends the meeting.'
quiz.answer: It is important that he attend the meeting.
quiz.explain: Formal subjunctive me ('essential/important/necessary' ke baad) verb hamesha BASE FORM rehta hai, 's' nahi lagta chahe subject third-person singular ho.

quiz.q: Fill (formal subjunctive with 'suggest'): 'The doctor suggested that she ___ (rest) for a few days.'
quiz.explain: 'Suggest' ke baad bhi base form aata hai — 'rest', 'rests' ya 'rested' nahi, chahe sentence past me ho.

quiz.q: Fill (annoyance/complaint about someone else's habit): 'I wish he ___ (not/interrupt) me while I'm speaking.'
quiz.explain: Doosron ke irritating behaviour pe complain karne ke liye 'I wish' + would/wouldn't use hota hai.

quiz.q: Why is 'God bless you' grammatically unusual — what would the 'normal' (non-subjunctive) form be?
quiz.answer: Normal third-person singular form would be 'God blesses you' — but as a fixed subjunctive expression (a wish/prayer), it stays in the base form 'bless'.
quiz.explain: Fixed subjunctive expressions like 'God bless you', 'Long live the King' use the base verb form because they express a wish/prayer, not a factual statement.

quiz.q: Which type of Subjunctive is used in 'It is necessary that she attend the meeting'?
quiz.explain: Present Subjunctive uses the BASE FORM of the verb after words like essential/necessary/important/suggest/recommend/demand — despite the name 'present', it has no connection to tense.

quiz.q: Which type of Subjunctive is used in 'I wish I were taller'?
quiz.explain: Past Subjunctive is the 'were' form used in unreal wishes, 'as if/as though', and Type 2 Conditionals — called 'past' because 'were' is grammatically a past form, even though the sentence is about the present.

quiz.q: Correct the error: 'It is imperative that everybody comes on time.'
quiz.answer: It is imperative that everybody come on time.
quiz.explain: 'Imperative' is an adjective trigger for the Present Subjunctive — the verb after 'that' stays in base form ('come'), never takes 's' even with 'everybody' (which feels singular).

quiz.q: Correct the error: 'The boss wants that everybody comes on time.'
quiz.answer: The boss wants that everybody come on time.
quiz.explain: 'Wants that' is a less common but valid Present Subjunctive trigger — same rule applies, base form after 'that', no 's'.

quiz.q: Fill (adjective trigger): 'It is ___ that she attend the interview tomorrow.' (choose an adjective meaning 'very important')
quiz.answer: vital / crucial / essential / important / imperative (any of these fit)
quiz.explain: All these adjectives trigger the Present Subjunctive pattern: It is + adjective + that + subject + base form.

quiz.q: Correct the error: 'The rule requires that every member signs the form.'
quiz.answer: The rule requires that every member sign the form.
quiz.explain: 'Require' is a verb trigger for Present Subjunctive — base form 'sign', not 'signs', even though 'every member' is singular.
